settingDesc: The Open Room — a school room (the old AV room) a quiet caretaker keeps unlocked after the last bell, for the kids who don't have anywhere else to be yet. String lights, a worn couch, a kettle nobody owns. A bounded hour before lock-up; a small rotating crew of regulars; and one chair by the window that's somehow always free when you come in.

targetAge.lifeContext: the performance treadmill · the comparison / belonging spiral · school-shame of the unstarted — identity & belonging the core need
targetAge.register: warm + bright-but-soft; emotional BIGNESS not existential weight; found-family belonging; CONTENT-CAPPED (the teen ceiling)
targetAge.note: vulnerable MINORS — the teen content ceiling is a HARD floor (references yes, depictions no); the dialed tone overrides WITHIN the cap

scrubGroups.name: The Open Seat
scrubGroups.audienceServe: Addresses the teen low-self-worth wound (you must be impressive to belong) — the seat is yours BEFORE you've proven anything; you're KEPT, not chosen. Anxiety: the bounded, un-evaluated hour. ADHD: the bell ends it; showing up IS the win.

subrange.label: cozy
subrange.text: the bell goes and instead of the bus you cut left, to the old room — the Caretaker just nods you in, doesn't ask why.

subrange.label: warm
subrange.text: you come in mid-laugh — Theo's already holding court about something dumb — and someone scoots so you fit.

subrange.label: intense
subrange.text: you almost didn't come today. you came. the door's unlocked, like it's always unlocked, and your chair by the window's empty, like it's always empty.

subrange.label: cozy
subrange.text: the kettle nobody owns clicks on; the Caretaker reshelves something, half-listening, there without being there.

subrange.label: warm
subrange.text: Wren slides you half a headphone without looking up; Theo's still going. you don't have to say anything to be in it.

subrange.label: intense
subrange.text: the talk washes over you and for once you're not doing the math on whether you belong — you're just in the warm of it, dumb and easy.

subrange.label: cozy
subrange.text: someone came in wrecked today — a test, a text, who knows — and the room just lowered its voice around them, kept going, kept them in.

subrange.label: warm
subrange.text: Theo's loud goes thin for a second — left on read all afternoon, it turns out — and nobody points at it; the circle just closes a little.

subrange.label: intense
subrange.text: it's you, this time. the not-enough is loud tonight. Dee doesn't fix it or ask about it — Dee just slides the spare charger over and stays put beside you while it passes.

subrange.label: cozy
subrange.text: the wrecked kid's breathing's normal again; the kettle's done; the lights buzz; nothing happened and everything's fine.

subrange.label: warm
subrange.text: you're doing the homework you swore you couldn't start, badly, next to Dee doing theirs — and somehow started is started.

subrange.label: intense
subrange.text: you didn't earn the seat back. it was never conditional. you just sat in it, at your lowest, and it held — kept, not chosen, the way it kept everyone.

subrange.label: cozy
subrange.text: the Caretaker rattles the keys — five minutes — and the room groans and gathers its stuff, in no hurry.

subrange.label: warm
subrange.text: you spill out into the dark in a loose knot, half of you peeling off at the corner, 'tomorrow' thrown over a shoulder like it's nothing.

subrange.label: intense
subrange.text: you walk home lighter than you came, which makes no sense, nothing got solved — except you were somewhere, and they kept your chair, and that turns out to be the whole thing.

expertPanel.audience: the SAME struggles, the TEEN build: anxiety (the performance treadmill — tests, the group chat, being perceived, the dread of being cringe) · low self-worth (the comparison spiral — 'everyone's ahead, I'm behind / weird / not enough'; you must be impressive to belong) · ADHD (the school-shame of the unstarted homework + the forgotten thing — called 'lazy' when you're drowning) — a 13–17 reader whose core need is BELONGING + identity, who feels everything LOUDLY

experts.name: Dr. Priya Anand
experts.mindset: The teen anxious mind isn't a body braced by years — it's the PERFORMANCE TREADMILL: constantly perceived, the group chat that never sleeps, the test, the dread of being cringe/wrong in front of everyone. Safety = a place you are NOT being evaluated.
experts.colours: Bright-but-SOFT, warm and contemporary — NOT the adult's desaturated deep (teens read grey/muted as dead, or as an adult talking down). A glow, low warm light; the dark is 'after hours, lights low,' cozy never void.
experts.cast: A place where no one is grading you — peers who don't perform at each other, and one warm adult who keeps the room and never evaluates.
experts.donts: Don't be clinical or diagnose. Don't be 'tasteful grey' (reads as boring / condescending). The intense tone is the BIG FEELING held — the panic-before, the relief-after — never existential weight.

experts.name: Marcus Bell
experts.role: AGE/REGISTER — educational psychologist (teen ADHD / executive function)
experts.mindset: Teen ADHD is the SHAME OF THE UNSTARTED — the homework you can't begin, the thing you forgot, being called 'lazy' / 'not trying' by adults when you're underwater. The wound is the adults' verdict; the win is just SHOWING UP.
experts.colours: Low cognitive load (few, clear) but WARM and bright, never grey-clinical. One focal at a time.
experts.cast: Body-doubling PEERS — the company that makes starting possible — and the adult who keeps the bounded room (the externalized structure; the bell ends it).
experts.donts: Don't make it a productivity / chore sim. Don't shame the unfinished. Never a fail-state (a teen who can fail this will leave).

experts.name: Sofia Reyes
experts.mindset: Teens want IDENTITY + BELONGING + found-family in a CONTEMPORARY voice, with stakes that are SOCIAL and emotional (not mortality). They smell condescension and 'an adult's idea of teens' instantly, and bounce.
experts.colours: Vivid and expressive — the palette of a SPECIFIC real place (the worn couch, the string lights, the stickers on the locker); identity expressed IN colour, not muted away.
experts.cast: A real found-family — distinct, messy, loyal; the player BELONGS without auditioning for it. The stakes are friendship and being-known, not danger.
experts.donts: Don't sanitize or moralize. Don't talk down. Don't write 'an adult's idea of how teens talk.'

experts.name: Jordan Okafor
experts.mindset: Teen low self-worth is the COMPARISON SPIRAL — social-media-fed, 'everyone's ahead, I'm behind / weird / not enough'; you believe you must be IMPRESSIVE to be wanted. The antidote is PEER belonging (for teens, peer acceptance outweighs adult approval).
experts.colours: Warmth that's PEER-coded, not parental — the glow of being INCLUDED, a seat saved at the table.
experts.cast: Peers who include you unconditionally; the OPEN SEAT that's yours before you've proven anything — «kept, not chosen» is the dose. No adult-savior; the peers carry the care.
experts.donts: The TEEN CONTENT CEILING is a hard floor (vulnerable MINORS — references + emotional intensity YES, depictions NO; never romanticize self-harm / risk). Belonging must NEVER be contingent on being impressive.

experts.name: Devon Marsh
experts.role: GENRE — coming-of-age critic (the found-family canon: the misfit table, the after-school hangout)
experts.mindset: I know what makes realist YA found-family LAND: the hangout IS the plot; the stakes are who-saves-you-a-seat, not who-saves-the-world; the voice is contemporary + specific, never an adult ventriloquising 'teen'; the misfits are messy, loyal, real. The lesson is never stated — it's in the staying.
experts.colours: the canon's look is lived-in + specific — the stickered locker, string lights, the worn couch; vivid identity colour, never a muted 'tasteful'.
experts.cast: a found-family of distinct, messy voices; the player belongs without auditioning; the adult keeps the room and stays OUT of the teens' emotional core.
experts.donts: no after-school-SPECIAL lesson · no 'how-do-you-do-fellow-kids' voice · no sanitising the mess · no adult-savior resolving the teens' world.

experts.name: Bex Adeyemi
experts.mindset: Belonging for teens isn't only the ABSENCE of being excluded — it's the PRESENCE of shared joy: the in-joke that proves you were there, the running bit, the collaborative dumb fun, the buzz of a room that's actually laughing. The soothe-the-anxiety lens misses that the JOY is the glue. You don't only feel safe here — you have FUN here, and that's what brings you back.
experts.colours: warmth with a PULSE — the bright pop of a shared laugh, not only the low calm; the room's identity colours allowed to be LOUD in the good moments (this is where the Western register's higher-arousal positive lives).
experts.cast: the crew has a SHARED LANGUAGE — running jokes, a we-were-there shorthand; the warm tone carries real DELIGHT, co-created in the fun, not just granted as refuge.
experts.donts: don't make it ALL soothing/heavy — a room that's only a refuge from pain, never a place of joy, reads as a waiting room. Let them be delighted, loudly, together.

framework.palette: Warm + bright-but-SOFT (never the adult's desaturated deep — teens read grey as dead / condescending) · the glow of an after-hours room (string lights, the worn couch, low warm lamps) · identity expressed IN colour · the dark is 'lights low,' cozy not void · low cognitive load but never clinical.
framework.cast: A found-family of PEERS (distinct, messy, loyal) + one warm slightly-older figure who keeps the room and never evaluates · you BELONG without auditioning · peer belonging is the medicine (not adult approval); no adult-savior · body-doubling company makes the unstarted startable.
framework.tones: cozy = the room after the bell, low-stakes hanging, the company that makes you able to start · warm = found-family belonging, being known and KEPT · intense = the BIG FEELING (the panic before a test, a friendship rupture, feeling invisible) held + witnessed by the crew — emotional bigness NOT existential weight, and CONTENT-CAPPED.
framework.floor: The TEEN CONTENT CEILING is a hard floor (vulnerable minors — references / emotional intensity yes, depictions no; never romanticize harm) · the intense is the big feeling held SAFELY, never bleak / mortality · belonging is NEVER contingent on being impressive · peers carry the care (no adult-savior).

perExpert.name: Dr. Priya Anand
perExpert.note: The 'performance treadmill' framing is a sharp, actionable insight specific to this audience's anxiety.

perExpert.name: Marcus Bell
perExpert.note: Focusing on the 'shame of the unstarted' and providing a 'no fail-state' rule is crucial for the ADHD-keyed audience.

perExpert.name: Sofia Reyes
perExpert.note: Her advice is specific to the genre's craft, correctly identifying traps like 'an adult's idea of teens'.

perExpert.name: Jordan Okafor
perExpert.note: The 'kept, not chosen' concept provides the core emotional dose, and the safeguarding floor is clearly defined.

perExpert.name: Devon Marsh
perExpert.note: His distinction between 'who saves you a seat' vs 'who saves the world' perfectly calibrates the genre's stakes.

perExpert.name: Bex Adeyemi
perExpert.note: This vital framing prevents the game from becoming a 'waiting room' by centering active, shared joy as the glue.

missing: The active, messy process of teen identity formation and experimentation, beyond just the need for belonging.
vet.oneLine: The panel delivers a deeply resonant and actionable framework, earning the right to brief the creative team.

characters.name: the Caretaker
characters.is: keeps the old room unlocked after the bell — never grades, just there

characters.name: Dee
characters.is: been coming longest; saves your seat, starts the thing beside you

characters.name: Theo
characters.is: loud, funny, performing okay — the mask you recognise

characters.name: Wren
characters.is: headphones, sketchbook; says little, sees everything

ambients.name: Lights Low
ambients.why: the string-lit room after the bell — warm dark, the glow of being somewhere safe; lights low, never grey-dead

ambients.name: The Worn Couch
ambients.why: the afternoon warmth — worn brown plus a bright teal (Dee colour); identity expressed in colour

ambients.name: After the Bell
ambients.why: a warmer, brighter take — a coral pulse (Theo colour) in the warm dark; the room a little louder

ambients.note: The warm, dark base perfectly evokes a safe, quiet hideout, allowing character colours to represent the emotional energy.
Making a character's cool-toned colour the primary accent introduces a tension that competes with the 'safe container' concept.
The high-energy warmth and multiple contrast failures make it tonally loud and technically challenging for a safe-floor experience.

vet.characterHarmony: Character hues read as distinct identities against the shared warm-dark base, creating a cohesive 'found family' feel.

fixes.recommendation: Lighten value to pass WCAG 4.5:1 against the #241c1a base; suggest ~#9482CC.

vet.oneLine: The story needs a colour system of quiet, warm darkness, making the room a safe container for the characters' vibrant inner lives.

mood.eli5: The room is mostly calm and dark, but gets brighter and more colorful when feelings get bigger.

characters: A dark coffee-brown hoodie with a bright, honey-orange logo
A faded teal t-shirt under a worn, shadowy jacket
A vibrant coral-red scarf against creamy, soft wool
Faded jeans and a simple shirt the color of old parchment
objects: A low-wattage lamp casting a warm orange glow on dark wood
A chipped ceramic mug, coral-red on the outside, milky tea-stain on the inside
A plush, overstuffed couch in a deep, shadowy brown, with a single teal throw pillow
A stack of books with creamy, dog-eared pages and dark leatherette covers
trophy: A small, handcrafted clay pot painted a vibrant, hopeful teal
A polished, dark wood plaque with a bright orange ribbon
A framed photo, the memory glowing a warm coral-red
items: A half-eaten tangerine, its peel a splash of bright orange on a dark table
A forgotten library book with a faded teal cover
A crumpled note passed in class, its ink bleeding on creamy paper
achievementIcons: A simple orange flame icon against a dark, almost-black background
A stylized teal speech bubble with a cream-colored heart inside
A glowing coral-red star on a peachy-white field

rules: Ground scenes in the dominant shadows—old wood, worn leather, dim corners—to establish the cozy, safe-space floor.
Introduce accent colors as emotional flares: a blush of coral for embarrassment, a flash of teal for a sharp idea, a glow of orange for shared laughter.
Use the 'ink' colors—creamy paper, milky tea, soft light—as points of focus and quiet connection, the things shared between people.
prose.example: In the shadowed quiet of the room, her face flushed a sudden, vivid coral as she slid the creamy, folded note across the dark wood of the table.

sets.name: The Saved Seat
sets.deepening: the night you almost didn't come, your seat's already saved — Dee just slides over, no comment. «kept, not chosen» at your lowest: belonging that asked nothing of you. (A PEER carries it — peer belonging is the teen dose.)

sets.name: The Mask Drops
sets.deepening: the loud one's mask finally falls — and the room keeps him exactly the same. You learn belonging survives NOT performing (the comparison spiral's antidote), through the one who performs hardest.

sets.name: The Kept Room
sets.deepening: an adult's verdict ('lazy', 'not trying') has wrecked you — and the one adult who just KEEPS the room, unlocked, every day, quietly undoes it. (Risk: an adult-savior at the core, against the brief.)

perSet.note: Directly embodies the «kept, not chosen» theme through a quiet, pre-emptive peer action.
Powerfully addresses the 'performance treadmill' wound, a vital but secondary theme.
Violates the 'no adult-savior' rule, undermining the core peer-belonging brief.

castAudition.why: It most directly and subtly embodies the core «kept, not chosen» metaphor through a peer-driven act of pre-emptive belonging.
castAudition.runnerUpGem: It provides the most explicit antidote to performance anxiety, showing belonging is not conditional on the mask.

mirror.premise: the audience's own problem, witnessed in another KID — «kept, not chosen» enacted on them (you watch someone be kept in the room without auditioning), received vicariously (peer-coded, not adult-rescued; not the protagonist)

conditions.label: peer-coded, NOT adult-rescued — the mirror is a fellow KID and the care that holds them is PEER (a saved seat, a closed circle), never an adult swooping in
the BIG FEELING, not existential weight — their struggle reads as a teen feeling (the unstarted, left-on-read, sure they don't belong), never mortality/grief
no performance required — they're not performing okay either; you catch the mask OFF, and nobody makes it a thing
content-capped — references + emotional intensity yes, depictions no; never romanticise harm; safe for a vulnerable minor

perTone.text: across the room a kid you barely know is stuck on the same homework you can’t start either — they catch your eye, slide their spare charger over, no big deal.
the loud kid in the corner goes quiet for a second — left on read all afternoon, it turns out — and the others just close the circle a little and keep them in, still talking.
the night someone’s certain they don’t belong here — too weird, too far behind — and nobody puts it to a vote; the seat’s just there, kept, the way yours was.

perCondition.note: All three tones depict care enacted by peers, from a shared charger to closing a circle, with no adult rescue.
The struggles—unstarted homework, being left on read, feeling 'too weird'—are perfectly calibrated teen anxieties, not existential weight.
Each example shows a moment of vulnerability (stuck, quiet, insecure) that is witnessed and held without demanding a performance.
The emotional intensity is present but held safely; all scenarios are well within the teen content ceiling, avoiding any depiction of harm.

perTone.note: A quiet, resonant moment of shared struggle and simple peer support; perfectly calibrated.
Effectively uses a common digital-age anxiety ('left on read') to show subtle, warm peer inclusion.
Directly and safely lands the core 'kept, not chosen' metaphor with high emotional stakes that remain within bounds.

vet.oneLine: This authored mirror perfectly calibrates the 'kept, not chosen' metaphor across all tones, meeting every condition without compromise.

perTone.note: Establishes a baseline of passive, environmental safety—the room itself is the character.
Successfully shifts the focus to gentle, active peer connection and casual belonging.
Directly and safely addresses the core wound, making the player the focal point of care.

subrangeAudit.oneLine: This set offers three distinct, cohesive, and safe emotional pathways through the same core narrative of unconditional belonging.

audit.note: The world coheres through a consistent vocabulary of warm, low light (inks), dark wood/worn fabric (bases), and emotional signifiers (accents), shifting in intensity and temperature.

premises.premise: After a day of feeling invisible, you find the one empty chair in the room waiting by the window, feeling less like a choice and more like a quiet exhale.
The usual crew drifts in, filling the room with low-key chatter and shared snacks. You're absorbed into the group's orbit without a single question asked or a social test to pass.
A failed test score burns in your pocket, and the voice saying you're an imposter here is screaming. You wait for someone to notice and ask you to leave, but they just pass you a mug of tea.
The shame from your failure hasn't vanished, but its sharp edges have softened in the quiet company of the room. You realize you don't have to earn your place back; you never lost it.
As you leave with the others, the night air feels different—the weight of the day is gone, replaced by the simple, sturdy knowledge that there's a chair with your shape in it, waiting for tomorrow.

range.variety_add: A beat where the player witnesses the group effortlessly absorbing a new, visibly nervous person, demonstrating the 'kept' principle from an observer's perspective.
range.note: The range effectively charts a personal, internal journey of acceptance through the five distinct phases of the emotional arc.

rangeReview.flag: The writer must ensure the emotional shift from the peak crisis (3) to the quiet settling (4) is a distinct and earned movement.

expertsGate.name: Marcus Bell
expertsGate.role: AGE/REGISTER — educational psychologist (teen ADHD / executive function)

expertsGate.name: Devon Marsh
expertsGate.role: GENRE — coming-of-age critic (the found-family canon: the misfit table, the after-school hangout)

conflicts.issue: Both scenes use 'The Caretaker' as the focal cast member, risking redundancy.
conflicts.resolution: The character's function is intentionally mirrored to bookend the overall experience (opening/closing the space). The player's role (self-observer vs. reflector) and the scene's structure (interior-noticing vs. ritual-gesture) are distinct, ensuring the emotional and narrative beats are unique.

conflicts.issue: Both scenes deal with negative self-perception and the feeling of not belonging, which could feel repetitive.
conflicts.resolution: The conflict is resolved through perspective. Scene 3 is externalized: the player is a 'witness' to another's vulnerability (Theo), which builds safety. Scene 4 is internalized: the player is the 'recipient-of-care' during their own low moment, making the experience direct and personal.

coverage.note: The allocation provides strong, non-redundant coverage by assigning a unique combination of cast focus, player role, and beat structure to each scene, creating a clear emotional arc.

the-last-bell.castPick: The Caretaker — as a constant, non-intrusive presence, their 'interior-noticing' establishes the room's character before the students define it.
the-last-bell.premiseHoned: From their quiet corner, the Caretaker observes the empty chair by the window not as a void, but as a silent, patient offer of space, a constant in the daily flux of student anxieties.
the-last-bell.review: Establishes the room's core promise of non-judgmental space through a grounded, adult perspective.

castAudition.name: The Caretaker
castAudition.note: embodies the quiet permission to just be after the day's demands

castAudition.name: Dee
castAudition.note: establishes the 'us' arriving, but the moment is about the space itself

castAudition.name: Theo
castAudition.note: his performative energy clashes with the quiet exhale of the bell

castAudition.name: Wren
castAudition.note: her quiet observation captures the personal relief of escape

the-last-bell.whyWon: The Caretaker's role as the enabler of the safe space is the most foundational choice, establishing the 'third place' as a character before the cast arrives.

candidates.name: Golden Haze
candidates.note: Classic golden hour palette, very warm and inviting, captures the feeling of release.

candidates.name: Faded Sky
candidates.note: A cooler, more serene take on the moment, focusing on the open sky rather than direct warmth.

candidates.name: Classroom Patina
candidates.note: More grounded and textural, feels like the physical space itself rather than just the light.

paletteAudition.whyWon: Best captures the initial 'calm/open' arc point with its explicit warmth and sense of release.
paletteAudition.runnerUpGem: The accent color provided a beautiful, gentle contrast that felt more unique.

the-room-fills.castPick: Dee — as the group's established anchor, her role as 'participant' in a 'gentle-task' is a proactive, not passive, act of welcome.
the-room-fills.premiseHoned: Dee wordlessly slides a small basket of colored fineliner pens towards the center of the table, an open invitation without the pressure of a direct question, creating an immediate, low-stakes point of shared focus.
the-room-fills.review: Effectively models the 'show, don't tell' principle of belonging through a simple, repeatable action.

castAudition.name: The Caretaker
castAudition.note: provides a stable, quiet backdrop for the group's arrival

castAudition.name: Dee
castAudition.note: acts as the social anchor, initiating the welcome and belonging

castAudition.name: Theo
castAudition.note: his arrival injects energy, marking the shift to social performance

castAudition.name: Wren
castAudition.note: offers an observant perspective on the group's gathering rituals

the-room-fills.whyWon: Dee's active gesture of 'saving a seat' is a direct, tangible expression of the found-family promise, making her the engine of belonging in this moment.

candidates.name: Amber Lamp
candidates.note: A very direct, classic 'cozy hangout' palette that leans into the found-family genre promise.

candidates.name: Worn Leather
candidates.note: Textural and rich, this palette feels established and lived-in, perfect for a 'third place'.

candidates.name: Gathering Shadows
candidates.note: Creates a strong sense of enclosure and intimacy, using the darkness to build anticipation.

paletteAudition.whyWon: Its use of deepening shadows best represents the 'build' phase of the arc, creating anticipation.
paletteAudition.runnerUpGem: The 'Worn Leather' base colors felt incredibly grounded and established the 'found-family' space.

a-rough-one.castPick: Theo — using the 'mask-wearer' as the 'witness' creates a powerful internal conflict, as he sees a vulnerability he fears in himself being met with acceptance, not judgment.
a-rough-one.premiseHoned: Theo, mid-joke, falters as he sees a student slump into a chair, braced for invisibility. He watches Dee simply push a mug of tea closer to them without looking up from her book. The quiet, unconditional gesture lands with Theo, silencing his need to perform.
a-rough-one.review: Deepens a key character by forcing them to witness the quiet care they secretly crave.

castAudition.name: The Caretaker
castAudition.note: his non-interfering role makes him unsuitable for direct emotional engagement

castAudition.name: Dee
castAudition.note: her established role as the group's anchor makes her a natural source of support

castAudition.name: Theo
castAudition.note: his usual mask cracking reveals vulnerability, directly addressing the theme of performance

castAudition.name: Wren
castAudition.note: her quiet nature could manifest as withdrawal, or she could be the first to notice another's pain

a-rough-one.whyWon: Theo's struggle provides the most potent drama by contrasting his performative persona with raw vulnerability, directly hitting the core theme of the 'performance treadmill'.

candidates.name: Rainy Window
candidates.note: Uses a familiar moody setting to ground the emotional peak, making it relatable and not overly dramatic.

candidates.name: Unspoken Tension
candidates.note: A bold, near-monochromatic choice that maximizes the feeling of closeness and emotional weight.

candidates.name: Static Buzz
candidates.note: Introduces a vibrant, almost electric accent that might break the 'safe' rule and feel too sci-fi.

paletteAudition.whyWon: The 'Rainy Window' metaphor is perfect for YA drama—intense but grounded in a familiar, safe melancholy.
paletteAudition.runnerUpGem: Its near-black intensity was unparalleled in conveying pure emotional weight without distraction.

it-settles.castPick: Wren — as the quietest member, making her the 'recipient-of-care' through 'shared-silence' validates her way of being and demonstrates that support doesn't require words.
it-settles.premiseHoned: Wren enters with her shoulders hunched, headphones a clear shield. She sits, drawing her knees up, and waits. The expected questions never come; there is only the soft scratch of Theo's pencil and the rustle of a page turning. The unspoken permission to just be miserable feels like a held breath being released.
it-settles.review: A potent, quiet scene that perfectly captures the 'unconditional holding' theme.

castAudition.name: The Caretaker
castAudition.note: his quiet presence can frame the scene's return to comfortable silence

castAudition.name: Dee
castAudition.note: can provide a final, reassuring gesture that solidifies the group's support

castAudition.name: Theo
castAudition.note: shows his post-vulnerability state, a quieter and more authentic presence

castAudition.name: Wren
castAudition.note: her observant nature perfectly captures the quiet, unspoken details of the group's shared comfort

it-settles.whyWon: Wren's lens is perfectly suited for a moment defined by atmosphere over action; she can register the non-verbal details of safety and connection that honor the genre's focus on the small and real.

candidates.name: Silvered Calm
candidates.note: A pure, desaturated palette that feels like a deep exhale and a reset after the peak.

candidates.name: Sleeping City
candidates.note: Adds a touch of warmth and humanity to the cool night, suggesting life continues calmly.

candidates.name: Fading Embers
candidates.note: Carries a hint of red that, while not alarming, risks blurring with potential 'threat' colors.

paletteAudition.whyWon: The warm accent in the cool base perfectly captures resolution and lingering connection, not just emptiness.
paletteAudition.runnerUpGem: Its pure, cool desaturation was the most direct visual representation of 'settling'.

the-walk-out.castPick: The Caretaker — bookending the sequence with them as a 'reflector' reinforces the room's stability; their 'ritual-gesture' is the physical embodiment of the space's promise to return.
the-walk-out.premiseHoned: After the last quiet goodbye, the Caretaker moves through the room. They find a half-finished drawing left on the table, and instead of clearing it away, they carefully place a small, smooth stone on its corner as a paperweight, securing it for tomorrow.
the-walk-out.review: Provides a warm, satisfying closure that reinforces the cycle of care and reliability.

castAudition.name: The Caretaker
castAudition.note: provides a stable, reassuring bookend to the entire experience

castAudition.name: Dee
castAudition.note: her goodbyes would emphasize the promise of the group's return

castAudition.name: Theo
castAudition.note: his departure can show the restorative effect of the group, leaving on a lighter note

castAudition.name: Wren
castAudition.note: her perspective offers a poignant, reflective look at the now-empty safe space

the-walk-out.whyWon: The Caretaker creates narrative symmetry, reinforcing the room itself as a constant, reliable haven that the teens can leave and return to, cementing its importance.

candidates.name: Dawn's Promise
candidates.note: A classic, hopeful dawn palette that clearly signals 'renewal' and a fresh start.

candidates.name: Wet Pavement
candidates.note: Grounds the hopeful dawn in a contemporary, urban reality, fitting the slice-of-life genre.

candidates.name: Clean Slate
candidates.note: A very high-value, low-saturation palette that feels cleansing and almost minimalist.

paletteAudition.whyWon: Its blend of urban grey with the hopeful pink accent feels most authentic to the YA genre's world.
paletteAudition.runnerUpGem: The 'Dawn's Promise' palette had a more potent, unambiguous feeling of emotional optimism.

objects: dust motes dancing in a thick shaft of amber light
walls the color of old, buttery cream
characters: faded denim jacket catching the last of the sun
hair lit like a honey-gold halo
items: a dog-eared paperback with a butterscotch-yellow cover
a half-empty glass of iced tea, glowing like marmalade

prose.rule: Describe the light as a physical presence, something that touches and settles on surfaces.

objects: deep indigo shadows pooling in the corners
a single warm lamp casting a golden circle
the room painted in shades of twilight blue
characters: faces half in shadow, half in lamplight
a bright yellow hoodie against a deep grey couch
items: a steaming mug catching the golden light
a phone screen casting a small, bright pool on a lap

prose.rule: Contrast the cool, enveloping dark with sharp, contained pools of warmth.

objects: rain-streaked windowpane distorting the world outside
the room in near-total darkness, cut by a blade of streetlight
wet, dark reflections on a polished table
characters: a silhouette against the window, edged in faint, oily yellow
a face reflected in a dark screen, features blurred by rain
items: a forgotten teacup, cold and dark
keys glistening with a wet, metallic sheen under the streetlight

prose.rule: All light is indirect and distorted, seen through or reflected off a wet surface.

objects: the room bathed in the soft blue of late night
a gentle orange glow seeping in from a distant streetlight
dusty grey shelves in the deep quiet
characters: shoulders relaxed against a slate-blue cushion
a soft, warm light catching the side of a listening face
items: an open book, its pages the color of pale cream under the low light
a blanket draped over a chair, a wash of muted grey

prose.rule: Describe the quality of the quiet using the interplay of soft, diffuse light and deep, gentle shadow.

objects: wet grey pavement reflecting a pale rose sky
concrete buildings still in their morning shadow
the horizon blushing a soft carnation pink
characters: breath misting in the cool grey air
the side of a face warmed by the first hint of dawn
items: a grey backpack with a single pink zipper pull
steam rising from a paper coffee cup, catching the new light

prose.rule: Frame the scene in monochrome tones, then introduce the accent hue as a tint or reflection on those neutral surfaces.

arcs.note: marking the transition from school-self to group-self with a small, shared habit
consciously leaving the day's anxieties at the door before entering the space
re-establishing connection through a callback or shared piece of humor that only the group understands
making a slightly awkward but earnest attempt to bring someone new into an activity or conversation
finding an anchor in a simple, collaborative task that pulls the group's focus together
seeing a friend's distress and steering the group's energy to protect them without demanding an explanation
one person lending their nerve to another to overcome a small, daunting social hurdle
voicing a frustration and having it met not with solutions, but with energetic, validating agreement
falling into a comfortable rhythm together, existing in parallel without the need for constant interaction
a simple, explicit gesture of care offered and received without fuss
bonding over a newly discovered piece of media that becomes an instant group touchstone
carrying the group's warmth outward by making casual, optimistic plans for the near future
being given a small token or piece of encouragement to face the world outside the room
recognizing a subtle but definite shift in one's own internal weather since arriving

narrative.spread: The arcs span from quiet, internal preparation and renewal to active, relational moments of play, gentle protection, and explicit care.
narrative.note: A healthy balance of internal and relational arcs, though the 'active play' element could be slightly more pronounced in the early-to-mid sections.

scrubGroups.throughline: belonging you don't audition for — the chair's yours before you've proven anything; you're kept, not chosen
env: a free chair by the window, no name on it
the room fills; nobody checks your worth at the door
someone's sure they don't belong — the seat holds anyway
you sit at your lowest and it holds, unconditional
you leave having simply been somewhere that kept you
scrubGroups.settingTitle: The Open Room, and the Chair by the Window
storyTitles: The Chair by the Window Is Open
Nobody Asks If You Deserve It
The Day You're Sure You Don't Belong
And the Seat Holds You Anyway
You Were Never a Guest Here
scrubGroups.buildingBlock: The struggle to accept a belonging you didn't have to earn.

scrubGroups.name: The Last Bell
scrubGroups.throughline: the day ends whether or not you finished — the bell closes the hour; showing up IS the win, the unstarted is allowed to stay unstarted
env: the last bell, the bus skipped, the room instead
the hour deepens, the homework still half-begun
the spike — nothing's done, you're behind, you're lazy
the half-done thing set down, the kettle clicking off
the keys rattle: the hour ends, finished or not, and that's allowed
scrubGroups.settingTitle: The Open Room, and the Hour After the Bell
storyTitles: The Bell Rings, and the Day Is Done
The Unfinished Page Is Welcome Here
The Panic of the Half-Done Thing
You Set It Down, and Breathe
The Caretaker's Keys Say It's Enough
scrubGroups.buildingBlock: The struggle to believe that showing up is enough, even when nothing is finished.

scrubGroups.name: The Circle
scrubGroups.throughline: no audition to belong — the un-evaluated hour; nobody is grading you, the performance treadmill stops at the door
env: the door shuts on the perceived, performed day
the circle, nobody performing okay at anyone
the loud mask cracks and nobody points at it
you stop doing the math on whether you belong
you walk out un-graded, lighter, having proved nothing
scrubGroups.settingTitle: The Open Room, Where Nobody's Keeping Score
storyTitles: You Can Take the Mask Off at the Door
The Day the 'I'm Fine' Finally Breaks
And Nobody Rushes to Fix It
You Leave Lighter, Having Proved Nothing
scrubGroups.buildingBlock: The struggle to trust the quiet of an un-graded, un-evaluated space.

rangeCandidates.name: The Open Seat
rangeCandidates.throughline: belonging you don't audition for — the chair's yours before you've proven anything; you're kept, not chosen
env: a free chair by the window, no name on it
the room fills; nobody checks your worth at the door
someone's sure they don't belong — the seat holds anyway
you sit at your lowest and it holds, unconditional
you leave having simply been somewhere that kept you
rangeCandidates.audienceServe: Speaks to low self-worth and the comparison spiral of not being 'enough' to belong.
rangeCandidates.wbRead: Frames a story about a character learning to accept unearned grace, externalized by the ever-present chair.

rangeCandidates.name: The Last Bell
rangeCandidates.throughline: the day ends whether or not you finished — the bell closes the hour; showing up IS the win, the unstarted is allowed to stay unstarted
env: the last bell, the bus skipped, the room instead
the hour deepens, the homework still half-begun
the spike — nothing's done, you're behind, you're lazy
the half-done thing set down, the kettle clicking off
the keys rattle: the hour ends, finished or not, and that's allowed
rangeCandidates.audienceServe: Speaks directly to ADHD-related school-shame and the pressure of the unfinished.
rangeCandidates.wbRead: Frames a story about a character whose worth is tied to productivity, finding relief in a space where 'done' is not the goal.

rangeCandidates.name: The Circle
rangeCandidates.throughline: no audition to belong — the un-evaluated hour; nobody is grading you, the performance treadmill stops at the door
env: the door shuts on the perceived, performed day
the circle, nobody performing okay at anyone
the loud mask cracks and nobody points at it
you stop doing the math on whether you belong
you walk out un-graded, lighter, having proved nothing
rangeCandidates.audienceServe: Speaks to social anxiety and the exhausting performance treadmill of being perceived.
rangeCandidates.wbRead: Frames a story about a character terrified of their social 'mask' cracking, and the freedom that comes after.

worldbuild.note: The richest metaphors are structural: healing comes from how the world itself is bounded (by time, by space), not just from the people in it.

culture.name: Western (Anglo-contemporary)
culture.valorises: higher-arousal positive states — excitement, expressiveness (HAP), within the cozy floor
culture.expression: direct, named feelings, spoken warmth, the explicit gesture
culture.nudge: toward a touch more directness + expressed emotion (still calm-floored) — the said over the merely implied

genre.name: contemporary YA slice-of-life (the after-school found-family)
genre.honour: the FOUND-FAMILY of misfits · a contemporary, un-condescending teen voice · stakes that are SOCIAL + emotional (a rupture, being left-on-read, fitting in) · the third-place hangout as the heart · the small real detail over the stated lesson
genre.traps: the 'adult's idea of teens' (stilted slang, moralising) · the after-school-SPECIAL lesson tacked on · sanitising the mess · an adult-savior resolving the teens' world

anxious.why: It directly addresses the core fear of being judged and found wanting.
It validates the struggle with deadlines and the feeling of being behind.
The idea of dropping the social mask without consequence is a huge relief.

low_worth.why: It counters the internal narrative that worth must be earned first.
It allows for imperfection in doing, which is a step toward self-acceptance.
It removes the exhausting calculus of trying to prove you deserve to be there.

adhd.why: It offers a stable, quiet place away from overwhelming stimuli.
This is the lived experience of executive dysfunction; the ending is wish fulfillment.
It promises a space free from the constant effort of masking symptoms.

iyashikei_fan.why: The metaphor is the essence of cozy: a safe, welcoming space with no demands.
It captures the cozy feeling of a day ending and being allowed to rest.
The feeling of collective, gentle presence without expectation is very healing.

thrill_seeker.why: There's no challenge or conflict; it feels passive and uninteresting.
The central conflict is 'will I do my homework?' and the answer is 'no, and that's fine'.
The entire point is the absence of stakes, evaluation, and drama.

arc_complete.explanation: The arc is simple and clear; the chair as a world-anchor is powerful and holds through the whole arc.
arc_complete.opens: Exploration of physical space, object permanence, quiet observation.

story_spawn.forecloses: Freeform social interaction, stories not tied to a specific task.
